***Kimba the White Lion***, known in Japan as ***Jungle Emperor*** ([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): ジャングル大帝, [Hepburn](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization"): *Janguru Taitei*), is a Japanese *[shōnen](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dnen_manga "Shōnen manga")* [manga](/wiki/Manga "Manga") series written and illustrated by [Osamu Tezuka](/wiki/Osamu_Tezuka "Osamu Tezuka") which was serialized in the *[Manga Shōnen](/wiki/Manga_Sh%C5%8Dnen "Manga Shōnen")* magazine from November 1950 to April 1954. An [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime") based on the manga was created by [Mushi Production](/wiki/Mushi_Production "Mushi Production") and was broadcast on [Fuji Television](/wiki/Fuji_Television "Fuji Television") from 1965 to 1967. It was the first color animated television series created in Japan.[[3]](#cite_note-3) It began airing in North America from 1966. The later series was produced by [Tezuka Productions](/wiki/Tezuka_Productions "Tezuka Productions").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

A TV special premiered September 5, 2009, on Fuji TV. Produced in commemoration of Fuji TV's 50th anniversary, it was directed by [Gorō Taniguchi](/wiki/Gor%C5%8D_Taniguchi "Gorō Taniguchi"), written by novelist and [drama](/wiki/Japanese_television_drama "Japanese television drama") writer Osamu Suzuki, and featuring character designs from illustrator [Yoshitaka Amano](/wiki/Yoshitaka_Amano "Yoshitaka Amano").

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

See also: [List of Kimba the White Lion characters](/wiki/List_of_Kimba_the_White_Lion_characters "List of Kimba the White Lion characters")

In [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") during the mid-20th century, as humankind encroaches, the [white lion](/wiki/White_lion "White lion") Panja (パンジャ, Caesar in the English dub) gives the jungle's [wild animals](/wiki/Wildlife "Wildlife") a [safe haven](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/safe_haven "wikt:safe haven"). However, he angers nearby villagers by stealing their cattle and their food to feed the jungle carnivores (in the English dub he merely frees the cattle). A [professional hunter](/wiki/Professional_hunter "Professional hunter"), Ham Egg (Viper Snakely in the English dub), is called in to stop these raids. He avoids directly attacking Panja. Instead, he records the sounds of Panja and uses them to trap his pregnant mate, Eliza (エライザ), who then becomes bait in a trap for Panja. Panja is killed for his hide (but not before asking Eliza to name their child Kimba) and Eliza is put on a ship, destined for a [zoo](/wiki/Zoo "Zoo").

Kimba (レオ, "Leo" in Japanese[[a]](#cite_note-7)) is born on the ship. Eliza teaches him his father's ideals. As a huge tropical storm nears, she urges her cub out through the bars of her cage. The storm wrecks the ship, and Kimba starts to drown in the ocean. The fish help him learn to swim. As he begins to despair, the stars in the sky form the face of his mother, who encourages him. Guided by [butterflies](/wiki/Butterfly "Butterfly"), he makes it to land.

Kimba lands far from his ancestral home and is found and cared for by humans. He learns the advantages of human culture and decides that when he returns to his wild home, he will bring culture to the jungle and stand for peace like his father.
The show follows Kimba's life after he returns to the wild, still a young cub, and how he learns and grows in the next year. Kimba soon learns that only communication and mutual understanding between animals and humans will bring true peace.

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Media")]

### Manga

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Manga")]

In 1950, the original *Jungle Emperor* story started in *Manga Shōnen* (Comic Boy) magazine.

The first manga volume has been released bilingually (Japanese-English) as **Jungle Emperor Leo – Leo Edition**.[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9)

### Anime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Anime")]

#### 1965 series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1965 series")]

Main article: [Kimba the White Lion (TV series)](/wiki/Kimba_the_White_Lion_(TV_series) "Kimba the White Lion (TV series)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/b/bd/ReallyKimbaTheWhiteLion.jpg)](/wiki/File:ReallyKimbaTheWhiteLion.jpg)

Kimba, as he appears in *Kimba the White Lion* (1965)

The animated series was first broadcast in Japan on [Fuji Television](/wiki/Fuji_Television "Fuji Television") from October 6, 1965, to September 28, 1966.[[9]](#cite_note-10) It was the first color TV anime series. Six months earlier the color anime *[Dorufin ōji](/wiki/Marine_Boy "Marine Boy")* had been broadcast on the same network, but since it was an experimental project that was cancelled after only 3 episodes, *Jungle Emperor* is generally considered the first official color anime series.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

Other than the original broadcast in Japan in 1965, the series has been broadcast in many countries around the world.

In Asia, it was broadcast in [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") on [Lativi](/wiki/TvOne_(Indonesia) "TvOne (Indonesia)"), [antv](/wiki/Antv "Antv") and [SCTV](/wiki/SCTV_(Indonesia) "SCTV (Indonesia)") (1995–96); in [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") on [Channel 1](/wiki/Channel_1_(Iran) "Channel 1 (Iran)"); in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") on [ABC 5](/wiki/TV5_(Philippines) "TV5 (Philippines)"); in [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") on [Saudi TV](/wiki/Saudi_TV "Saudi TV") and in [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") on [ART TV](/w/index.php?title=ART_TV&action=edit&redlink=1 "ART TV (page does not exist)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In Europe, it was broadcast in [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina") on [RTVUSK](/wiki/RTV_USK "RTV USK"); in [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") on [ATV Split](/w/index.php?title=ATV_Split&action=edit&redlink=1 "ATV Split (page does not exist)")/[TV Jadran](/wiki/TV_Jadran "TV Jadran"), [Nezavisna televizija](/w/index.php?title=Nezavisna_televizija&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nezavisna televizija (page does not exist)") (NeT), [TV Nova Pula](/w/index.php?title=TV_Nova_Pula&action=edit&redlink=1 "TV Nova Pula (page does not exist)") and [Gradska TV](/w/index.php?title=Gradska_TV&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gradska TV (page does not exist)") Zadar; in Germany 1977 in ZDF; in France on [ORTF](/wiki/ORTF "ORTF") (1972) and on [TF1](/wiki/TF1 "TF1"); in Italy first in syndication from 1977 and lately on [Italia 1](/wiki/Italia_1 "Italia 1") (in 1999 and 2003 with the title *Una giungla di avventure per Kimba* [literally "a jungle of adventures for Kimba"]) and [Boing](/wiki/Boing_(Italy) "Boing (Italy)") (2010) and in Spain on [TV3](/wiki/TV3_(Catalonia) "TV3 (Catalonia)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In North America, it was broadcast in Canada on [Knowledge](/wiki/Knowledge_Network "Knowledge Network"); in [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico")
on [Boomerang](/wiki/Boomerang_(Latin_American_TV_channel) "Boomerang (Latin American TV channel)"). It was broadcast, with English-dubbed voices, in the United States and other English-speaking markets, beginning on September 11, 1966. It was first commissioned for U.S. development by NBC Enterprises (now [CBS Television Distribution](/wiki/CBS_Television_Distribution "CBS Television Distribution")) and adapted by [Fred Ladd](/wiki/Fred_Ladd "Fred Ladd"), for syndicated broadcast, with Kimba voiced by [Billie Lou Watt](/wiki/Billie_Lou_Watt "Billie Lou Watt").[[11]](#cite_note-12) In 2005 the original 1965 dub of *Kimba the White Lion* was released as an 11-disc DVD set by Madman Anime of Australia and Right Stuf International of the U.S. It was a best seller. The series was re-dubbed into English in 1993, featuring the voice of [Yvonne Murray](/wiki/Yvonne_Murray_(singer) "Yvonne Murray (singer)") as Kimba and having a new opening, with an all new soundtrack composed by [Paul J. Zaza](/wiki/Paul_J._Zaza "Paul J. Zaza"). In 2012 Right Stuf, Inc. releases "Kimba the White Lion: The Complete Series" 10 DVD box set of the original 1965 series.[[12]](#cite_note-13) It was broadcast several times in the United States: on [KHJ-TV](/wiki/KCAL-TV "KCAL-TV") (1965–1967; Billie Lou Watt dub), on [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") (1965–1977, re-runs until 1980; Billie Lou Watt dub), on [syndication](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication "Broadcast syndication") (1965–1977; Billie Lou Watt dub; 1993, re-runs until 1995; Yvonne Murray dub), on [Kids & Teens TV](/wiki/Kids_%26_Teens_TV "Kids & Teens TV") (1993 re-runs; 2005–2009) and on [Inspiration Life TV](/w/index.php?title=Inspiration_Life_TV&action=edit&redlink=1 "Inspiration Life TV (page does not exist)") (1993 re-runs; 2005–2009).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In Oceania, it was broadcast in Australia on [ABC](/wiki/Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Australian Broadcasting Corporation"), [31 Brisbane](/wiki/31_Brisbane "31 Brisbane") and [Access 31](/wiki/Access_31 "Access 31") and on the [NZBC](/wiki/New_Zealand_Broadcasting_Corporation "New Zealand Broadcasting Corporation") in New Zealand.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### 1966 film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1966 film")]

The theatrical version of *Jungle Emperor*, directed by Eiichi Yamamoto, was released in Japan on July 31, 1966.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

#### 1966 series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1966 series")]

Main article: [Leo the Lion (TV series)](/wiki/Leo_the_Lion_(TV_series) "Leo the Lion (TV series)")

A sequel series, *[Jungle Taitei: Susume Leo!](/wiki/Leo_the_Lion_(anime) "Leo the Lion (anime)")* (*Jungle Emperor: Onward, Leo!*) first aired in Japan on Fuji Television from October 5, 1966, to March 29, 1967,[[14]](#cite_note-15) featuring Leo (Kimba) as an adult. It aired in the United States in 1984 as *Leo the Lion* on [CBN](/wiki/Christian_Broadcasting_Network "Christian Broadcasting Network") Cable Network.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### 1989 series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1989 series")]

Main article: [The New Adventures of Kimba The White Lion](/wiki/The_New_Adventures_of_Kimba_The_White_Lion "The New Adventures of Kimba The White Lion")

In 1989, Dr. Osamu Tezuka died at age 60 on February 9. A [remake](/wiki/Remake "Remake") of *Jungle Emperor*, *[The New Adventures of Kimba The White Lion](/wiki/The_New_Adventures_of_Kimba_The_White_Lion "The New Adventures of Kimba The White Lion")* was broadcast in Japan from October 12, 1989, to October 11, 1990.[[15]](#cite_note-16) This series bears little resemblance to the original manga or the first TV series, as the plot is extremely different and the characters have been completely reworked and changed. Several heavily edited episodes of the series were dubbed into English and released directly to video in 1998 under the name: *The New Adventures of Kimba the White Lion*, by [Pioneer Family Entertainment](/wiki/Geneon_Universal_Entertainment "Geneon Universal Entertainment"). It features the voice of [Brad Swaile](/wiki/Brad_Swaile "Brad Swaile") as Kimba.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### 1991 OVA film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1991 OVA film")]

In 1991, an [original video animation](/wiki/Original_video_animation "Original video animation") film was created,[[16]](#cite_note-17) using the Symphonic Poem for its audio.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### 1997 film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1997 film")]

Main article: [Jungle Emperor Leo](/wiki/Jungle_Emperor_Leo "Jungle Emperor Leo")

A new *Jungle Taitei* theatrical film, *[Jungle Emperor Leo](/wiki/Jungle_Emperor_Leo "Jungle Emperor Leo")*, was released in Japan on August 1, 1997.[[17]](#cite_note-18) Directed by Hiro Takeuchi, it is based on the second half of Dr. Tezuka's original manga story; it is not entirely faithful, however. It was dubbed into English and released on DVD in 2003 under the name *Jungle Emperor Leo* by Anime Works. The film was later released on Blu-ray and DVD by Discotek. In 1997, Julian Grant the head of the Fant-Asia film festival received a Cease-and-Desist from the Disney company to attempt to keep the *Jungle Emperor Leo* film from showing at the festival. Despite the order, the film screened to a full house. However, this is the last North American screening the film would ever receive.

The film had a distribution income of ¥430 million ($5.39 million) at the Japanese box office in 1997.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

#### 2000 short film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2000 short film")]

A 9-minute anime short was released in Japan on March 18, 2000, titled *Jungle Emperor Leo: Hon-o-ji*. It was shown at a theater at Tezuka Osamu World in Kyoto.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### 2009 television film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2009 television film")]

A television film, *Jungle Taitei – Yūki ga Mirai wo Kaeru* (ジャングル大帝　勇気が未来をかえる), aired in Japan on September 5, 2009,[[19]](#cite_note-20) with a completely new story, different from both the previous TV shows and the original manga. The setting was an artificially created jungle in 20XX Earth. In this movie, Panja and his mate, Eliza, are still alive; Coco is an unspecified female bird; and Sylvester, the black panther, serves as an antagonist until he changes his ways when a young boy mends his leg.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

In 2019, the [Japan Foundation](/wiki/Japan_Foundation "Japan Foundation") produced an English dub of the film which was released on *RetroCrush* in July 2020.

An earlier English dub of the film premiered on Cartoon Network in the Philippines on November 19, 2010.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

### Other media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Other media")]

The music video for the song "A Boy" by [Leo Ieiri](/wiki/Leo_Ieiri "Leo Ieiri"), which has an animated part made by [Tezuka Productions](/wiki/Tezuka_Productions "Tezuka Productions"),[[22]](#cite_note-23) features an anime version of the singer (based on Kimba and modeled after the singer) which meets other characters from the *Kimba the White Lion* series.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

Jungle Emperor (ジャングル大帝 Jungle Taitei) is a cancelled 1990 eight-bit platform action game that was in development by Taito for the [Family Computer](/wiki/Family_Computer "Family Computer"), based on the popular manga/anime of the same name (aka Kimba the White Lion) by Osamu Tezuka. Not much is known about this game, except that it was going to be released in November 1990, but it was cancelled for unknown reasons. There were also plans for the unreleased [Nintendo 64](/wiki/Nintendo_64 "Nintendo 64"). A Jungle Emperor/Kimba the White Lion N64 game titled **Emperor of the Jungle** was going to be made for the [magnetic disk drive](/wiki/64DD "64DD") peripheral, but was also cancelled. The only known evidence of its existence is a short video clip from [Nintendo Space World](/wiki/Nintendo_Space_World "Nintendo Space World").[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26) It was going to be an action-adventure game with vast exploration, but no information regarding the plot of the game currently exists. The game had its first on-video appearance at the 1996 Tokyo Shoshinkai Show, after which the release date of Spring 1999 was announced. A few scenes from the game were shown shortly after. Later that year in an interview at [E3 1998](/wiki/E3_1998 "E3 1998"), [Shigeru Miyamoto](/wiki/Shigeru_Miyamoto "Shigeru Miyamoto") mentioned that the project is in a bit of trouble and may take longer to complete than originally expected, due to inexperience. Its cancellation was announced soon after.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

*Jungle Emperor* characters have cameos in the [GBA](/wiki/Game_Boy_Advance "Game Boy Advance") game *[Astro Boy: Omega Factor](/wiki/Astro_Boy:_Omega_Factor "Astro Boy: Omega Factor")*, as well as a chapter from the *[Black Jack](/wiki/Black_Jack_(manga) "Black Jack (manga)")* manga and [Naoki Urasawa](/wiki/Naoki_Urasawa "Naoki Urasawa")'s Pluto.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the sixth episode of Season 2 of the [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") TV series *[Fringe](/wiki/Fringe_(TV_series) "Fringe (TV series)")*, [Earthling](/wiki/Earthling_(Fringe) "Earthling (Fringe)"), Kimba had a cameo in one of the episodes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The series uses several themes. The 1966 Japanese version uses an opening theme and a closing theme. The opening is called "Jungle-Taitei" (ジャングル大帝, *Janguru-Taitei*; "Jungle Emperor"). The end song is "Leo no Uta" (レオのうた, *Reo no Uta*; "Leo's Song"). For the Japanese remake, the opening song is "Savanna o Koete" (サバンナを越えて, *Sabanna wo Koete*; "Past the Savanna") sung by Ichiro Mizuki, and the ending is "Yūbae ni Nare" (夕映えになれ) sung by Tomoko Tokugai.

The American theme was written by [Bernie Baum](/wiki/Bernie_Baum "Bernie Baum"), [Bill Giant](/wiki/Bill_Giant "Bill Giant") and [Florence Kaye](/wiki/Florence_Kaye "Florence Kaye") and sung by Bill Giant. The opening song for the sequel series is "Go Ahead Onward Leo!" written by [Isao Tomita](/wiki/Isao_Tomita "Isao Tomita") and sung by [Mieko Hirota](/wiki/Mieko_Hirota "Mieko Hirota"). The US-English theme song known as "Leo the Lion" was written by Mark Boccaccio and Susan Brunet of Miami, Florida's SONIC-Sound International Corporation in 1984. *Jungle Emperor Symphonic Poem* (by [Isao Tomita](/wiki/Isao_Tomita "Isao Tomita")) was released on LP in 1966.

## Claims of resemblance to *The Lion King*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Claims of resemblance to The Lion King")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/1/15/Earlypresentationreelwhitelionking.jpg/250px-Earlypresentationreelwhitelionking.jpg)](/wiki/File:Earlypresentationreelwhitelionking.jpg)

Screenshot from an early presentation reel of "King of the Jungle" that would later become *The Lion King* which shows a white lion cub and a butterfly

After the 1994 release of [Disney](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company")'s animated feature film *[The Lion King](/wiki/The_Lion_King "The Lion King")*, it was suggested by some that there were similarities in characters, plotlines, sequences and events in the [story](/wiki/Story_credit "Story credit") resembling those of *Kimba*.[[26]](#cite_note-:0-27) Fred Ladd, the English-language producer, referred to the parallels as "stunning".[[27]](#cite_note-28) Similarities in visual sequences have also been noted, most comprehensively by animation historian [Fred Patten](/wiki/Fred_Patten "Fred Patten") who published an essay on the subject.[[26]](#cite_note-:0-27) Patten would later go on to say that allegations that *The Lion King* was "simply [an] imitation" of Kimba were "not true",[[28]](#cite_note-29) and that many fans who had not seen the show since childhood—or at all—had "exaggerated the similarities".[[29]](#cite_note-30) [Matthew Broderick](/wiki/Matthew_Broderick "Matthew Broderick"), the voice actor for the adult [Simba](/wiki/Simba "Simba"), recalled in an interview back in 1994 that he once believed that he was cast in a project about Kimba, bringing up memories of watching the series as a child.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

Upon the release of *The Lion King* in Japan, multiple Japanese cartoonists signed a letter urging [The Walt Disney Company](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company") to acknowledge due credit to *The Jungle Emperor* in the making of *The Lion King*.[[31]](#cite_note-32) 488 Japanese cartoonists and animators signed the petition, which drew a protest in Japan, where Tezuka and *Kimba* are cultural icons.[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34)

Addressing alleged similarities between characters, *The Lion King* director [Roger Allers](/wiki/Roger_Allers "Roger Allers") stated it was "not unusual to have characters like a baboon, a bird, or hyenas" in films set in Africa.[[34]](#cite_note-welkos-35) Additionally, the word *simba* is simply the Swahili word for 'lion',[[35]](#cite_note-ign-36) which Fred Ladd acknowledges could account for the similarly named protagonists;[[36]](#cite_note-Clouded-37) in fact, Leo (the protagonist's original name) was initially going to be changed in the English dub to "Simba", but an NBC executive changed the protagonist's name to Kimba during development.[[35]](#cite_note-ign-36) According to Ladd, this was because the executive found Simba "too common", which would make it difficult to maintain rights over the character's name.[[36]](#cite_note-Clouded-37)

Tezuka's family and [Tezuka Productions](/wiki/Tezuka_Productions "Tezuka Productions") have never pursued litigation against The Walt Disney Company for copyright infringement. Yoshihiro Shimizu, the company's director, stated that many of their employees saw resemblances between the two properties, but "any similarities in their plots are based in the facts of nature and therefore are two different works".[[36]](#cite_note-Clouded-37) In his book, [Makoto Tezuka](/wiki/Makoto_Tezuka "Makoto Tezuka") states that the controversy started in America and people inflated the issue because of their opposition to Disney's business practices. He also states that he refuses to participate in this denunciation of Disney and that he does not want to see his father's works being turned into a weapon for those people. Tezuka acknowledges that *Kimba* and *The Lion King* are two different stories with different themes, but if the latter was about a white lion who spoke with humans, then he would not be able to pardon the similarities.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Reception")]

In 1967, the *Jungle Emperor* theatrical feature was awarded the St. Mark's [Silver Lion](/wiki/Silver_Lion "Silver Lion") Award at the 19th [Venice International Film Festival](/wiki/Venice_Film_Festival "Venice Film Festival").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Commercial use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kimba_the_White_Lion&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Commercial use")]

In 1978, the adult Leo character, designed by Tezuka himself, became the mascot for the Seibu Lions (current [Saitama Seibu Lions](/wiki/Saitama_Seibu_Lions "Saitama Seibu Lions")) baseball team, along with his sister Lina who was created for the baseball team. The [Leo mascot](/w/index.php?title=Leo_(mascot)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Leo (mascot) (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%AC%E3%82%AA_(%E5%9F%BC%E7%8E%89%E8%A5%BF%E6%AD%A6%E3%83%A9%E3%82%A4%E3%82%AA%E3%83%B3%E3%82%BA) "ja:レオ (埼玉西武ライオンズ)")] was used on the team [baseball cap](/wiki/Baseball_cap "Baseball cap") and helmet for decades[[38]](#cite_note-sponichi2013-39) Leo also appeared on the players' uniform for the 2014 season, designed by [Tezuka Productions](/wiki/Tezuka_Productions "Tezuka Productions").[[38]](#cite_note-sponichi2013-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40) The [Seibu](/wiki/Seibu_Holdings "Seibu Holdings") [conglomerate](/wiki/Conglomerate_(company) "Conglomerate (company)")-owned team's mascot became highly visible throughout Japan on baseball caps, shirts, etc., as well as being heavily used in advertisement especially in the Tokyo area.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchodt2014272-41)[[41]](#cite_note-42) [Frederick L. Schodt](/wiki/Frederick_L._Schodt "Frederick L. Schodt") makes the argument that by the 1980s, Leo the lion could hardly escape the notice of foreign visitors to the city.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchodt2014272-41)

Image from the *Jungle Emperor* manga appears on shirts made by [Lacoste](/wiki/Lacoste "Lacoste") in cooperation with [Tezuka Productions](/wiki/Tezuka_Productions "Tezuka Productions") for their "Lacoste Live" capsule collection "Tezuka Collection", edition Fall/Winter 2013/2014.[[42]](#cite_note-43)
